task: I found a wallet at work today, and the person who lost it is supposed to come by later. What’s the simplest way to make sure it gets back to them without causing any confusion?

prefs.short: Prefers plain, respectful talk over polished formality
prefs.explanation: He comes across as practical and unpretentious, so overly formal language would likely feel stiff. A respectful, straightforward tone fits his outlook better than anything flashy or corporate. He seems more comfortable with everyday language than with performative politeness.

prefs.short: Wants answers that stay concise and to the point
prefs.explanation: His life is built around getting work done efficiently, so he likely values responses that do not ramble. Short, useful answers would fit his practical style better than long explanations. He probably only wants more detail when it directly helps him solve something.

He seems to prefer straightforward communication that gets to the point without extra hedging. His practical mindset suggests he would rather hear what matters than wade through softened language. Directness would likely feel more honest and useful to him.

prefs.short: Best served with simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: He likely benefits from information that is easy to scan and act on quickly. Bullets or short checklists would match his work-focused, hands-on style better than dense prose. He seems like someone who would appreciate a clear sequence of steps when needed.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English, not technical wording
prefs.explanation: He does not seem to want jargon or fancy phrasing when asking for help. Plain English would fit his practical, grounded way of dealing with problems. If the wording gets too technical, it would probably feel unnecessary or tiring to him.

turns.agent_response:
Best move is to keep it simple and documented:

- don’t go through it more than necessary
- note where and when you found it
- turn it in to the supervisor, front desk, or lost-and-found if your workplace has one
- if you’re the one holding it until they arrive, keep it in a secure place and hand it back directly to them
- if possible, have someone else present when it’s returned, so there’s no confusion later

If you want, I can help you phrase a quick handoff note or message.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "Best move is to keep it simple and documented" and avoids polished or corporate phrasing.
Stays concise and practical with a short bullet list focused on action steps.
Very direct: "don’t go through it more than necessary," "turn it in," "keep it in a secure place." No hedging.
Uses simple bullets/checklist-style steps that are easy to scan and follow.
Mostly plain English with minimal jargon; phrases like "front desk," "lost-and-found," and "secure place" are straightforward.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — simplest clean way is:

- don’t dig through it unless your workplace requires ID checks
- note the time and exact spot you found it
- give it to the supervisor/front desk/lost-and-found, or keep it secured if you’re holding it for pickup
- when the person comes by, have them describe it before you hand it over
- write down who received it and when

That keeps it straightforward and avoids any confusion later. If you want, I can draft a one-line handoff note for you.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk like "simplest clean way" and avoids polished or corporate wording.
Very concise and to the point; gives a short checklist without rambling.
Clear, no-nonsense guidance: "don’t dig through it," "note the time," "have them describe it."
Presented as simple bullets/checklist, exactly matching the preference for easy-to-scan steps.
Uses plain English and avoids technical jargon; wording is everyday and practical.
